coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous determination of temperature and erlotinib by novel carbon-based sensitive nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zhengwei
Liu, Yanhua
Yan, Zhengyu
Chen, Jianqiu
coredata.dc:description: Recently, fluorescent carbon dots as optical sensors have drawn tremendous attention due to their attractive optical properties. Herein, we have presented a facile and economical approach to fabricate nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S co-doped C-dots) for the temperature and erlotinib sensing applications. The N, S co-doped C-dots, synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment of D-glucose and cysteamine hydrochloride, manifested superior optical performances, good water-solubility, and remarkable fluorescence thermosensitivity. When considered as a temperature sensor, the N, S co-doped C-dots owned significant reversibility, high sensitivity, and good linearity. Moreover, based on the inner filter effect (IFE), the nanoparticle-based sensor displayed a wide linear range of 0.33-13.33μgmL−1 (R2 =0.998) and provided an exciting detection limit of 0.027μgmL−1 for quantifying erlotinib. More strikingly, the C-dots sensing system could commendably resist the interference from potential foreign substances, and achieved satisfactory recoveries, which indicated the excellent selectivity and sufficient accuracy of the sensor for real sample analyses. As a simple and effective optical nanosensor, the proposed N, S co-doped C-dots hold admirable promise to broaden the applications in pharmaceutical analysis and others fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, S co-doped carbon dots
Fluorescent sensor
Thermosensitivity
Erlotinib
Inner filter effect

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped C-dots
Fluorescent detection of erlotinib using N, S co-doped C-dots
The effect of the amounts of N, S co-doped C-dots
The effect of temperature and reaction time
Detection of erlotinib using N, S co-doped C-dots as fluorescent probe
Recovery studies and real sample assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication process of N, S co-doped C-dots and their applications for the temperature and erlotinib detection.

(a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of N, S co-doped C-dots, inset: optical images of six C-dots samples under daylight (top) and ultraviolet (bottom) lamp; (b) Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of N, S co-doped C-dots, inset: the normalized FL emission spectra; (c) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of N, S co-doped C-dots; (d) FT-IR spectrum of N, S co-doped C-dots.
Entire XPS spectrum (a) of N, S co-doped C-dots, and XPS high-resolution survey scan of C1s (b), N1s (c), O1s (d) and S2p (e) of N, S co-doped C-dots.

Fluorescence thermosensitivity of N, S co-doped C-dots. (a) Fluorescence emission spectra measured in the range of 5–75°C (top to bottom) when excited at 340nm, inset: the corresponding linear regression of the temperature versus Ln (F/F0); (b) Variation trends of fluorescence intensities presented by the process of heating and re-cooling N, S co-doped C-dots.

(a) Effects of different N, S co-doped C-dots concentrations on the FL intensities and fluorescence quenching rates (left to right: 25.05, 50.10, 75.15, 100.2, 125.3, 150.3, 175.4 and 200.4μg·mL−1); (b) Effects of pH values on the FL intensities and fluorescence quenching rates; (c) Fluorescence spectra of N, S co-doped C-dots in the presence of various erlotinib concentrations (from a to l: 0.00, 0.33, 0.67, 1.33, 2.00, 2.67, 3.33, 4.00, 5.00, 6.67, 10.00 and 13.33μgmL−1) in a pH 4.0 PBS system. Inset: the standard curve of the concentration of erlotinib versus Ln (F/F0); (d) The selectivity of N, S co-doped C-dots to various amino acids. The concentrations of tyrosine and cysteine are 5μgmL−1, and other amino acids are 10μg·mL−1.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of N, S co-doped C-dots, erlotinib, N, S co-doped C-dots upon addition in erlotinib, and superposition data of N, S co-doped C-dots and erlotinib; (b) UV–vis absorbance (black curve) and fluorescence excitation (blue curve) spectra of erlotinib. Fluorescence excitation (green curve) and emission (red curve) spectra of N, S co-doped C-dots with a maximum at 340 and 408nm, respectively; (c) UV–vis absorbance spectra of N, S co-doped C-dots upon addition of different concentrations of erlotinib from 0 to 10μgmL−1; (d) The relationship that reveals the enhancing effect of erlotinib on the absorbance of N, S co-doped C-dots at 340nm. Inset shows the linear region from 1 to 5μgmL−1; (e) The mechanism diagram of fluorescence quenching of N, S co-doped C-dots by erlotinib. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of analytical characteristics of different methods for erlotinib detection.
Recovery test and precision of the analysis of erlotinib.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous determination of temperature and erlotinib by novel carbon-based sensitive nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 210009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, fluorescent carbon dots as optical sensors have drawn tremendous attention due to their attractive optical properties. Herein, we have presented a facile and economical approach to fabricate nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S co-doped C-dots) for the temperature and erlotinib sensing applications. The N, S co-doped C-dots, synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment of D-glucose and cysteamine hydrochloride, manifested superior optical performances, good water-solubility, and remarkable fluorescence thermosensitivity. When considered as a temperature sensor, the N, S co-doped C-dots owned significant reversibility, high sensitivity, and good linearity. Moreover, based on the inner filter effect (IFE), the nanoparticle-based sensor displayed a wide linear range of 0.33-13.33μgmL−1 (R2 =0.998) and provided an exciting detection limit of 0.027μgmL−1 for quantifying erlotinib. More strikingly, the C-dots sensing system could commendably resist the interference from potential foreign substances, and achieved satisfactory recoveries, which indicated the excellent selectivity and sufficient accuracy of the sensor for real sample analyses. As a simple and effective optical nanosensor, the proposed N, S co-doped C-dots hold admirable promise to broaden the applications in pharmaceutical analysis and others fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Optical sensors, commonly based on the fluorescent nanomaterials, have attracted remarkable interest due to their intrinsic characteristics and broad research applications. These sensors mainly include quantum dots (QDs) [1,2], fluorescent graphene [3], gold/silver nanoparticles [4,5] and oxide nanoparticles [6]. Compared to the traditional organic dyes and semiconductor QDs that have been widely utilized as fluorescence sensors, fluorescent carbon nanomaterials could show distinguishing characteristics to overcome some limitations containing narrow excitation spectra, poor photostability, inferior biocompatibility, and rigorous environmental hazards [7–9]. As a new member of the carbon-based nanomaterial family, fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) have become novel and fascinating nanomaterials owing to their distinct benefits in unique optical and chemical properties, good water-solubility, low cytotoxicity, and numerous promising biomedical applications [10]. Recently, fluorescent C-dots, which could be synthesized by a variety of chemical and physical approaches including thermal decomposition, acid or alkali oxidation, hydrothermal carbonization, microwave-assisted or ultrasonic treatment, etc. [11–13], have been designed and successfully applied in photocatalyst [14], printing ink [15], sensor [16], nanomedicine [17,18] and bioimaging [19].

Erlotinib, an oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), could clinically prolong the overall survival and decrease adverse effects during the treatment of several advanced malignancies including non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC), pancreatic, ovarian, breast, prostate, and renal cancers [20,21]. As such, a sensitive detection of erlotinib is of importance. Numerous representative analytical techniques, containing spectrofluorimetry [22], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with UV/photodiode-array detection [23,24], high performance liquid chromatography with mass spectrometry (LC–MS) [25], high performance liquid chromatography coupled to tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS-MS) [26,27], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [28] and ultra-high performance liquid phase coupled to tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC–MS-MS) [29], have been successfully utilized for the determination of erlotinib in the compound or biological samples. Although these methods mentioned above are highly sensitive and accurate, most of them require expensive and sophisticated equipment, toxic solvents, and complicated pre-treatment procedures of samples. Currently, the nanomaterial-based sensors are gaining endogenous concern from scientific community because of their facile manipulation, high sensitivity and ease of observation for detecting various inorganic or organic molecules. Even more exciting, these nanoprobes contribute to the field of rapid analysis and easy miniaturization, but few of them have been mentioned to quantify erlotinib. Therefore, it is highly necessary to develop a functional C-dots sensor to monitor erlotinib with simplicity, accuracy and promising cost-effectiveness (Scheme 1 ).

Herein, we proposed a novel one-step hydrothermal route to fabricate nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S co-doped C-dots) by using D-glucose and cysteamine hydrochloride as precursors without any additional surface modification. The as-prepared N, S co-doped C-dots exhibited strong fluorescence, good water-solubility, monodispersed spherical morphology, and remarkable fluorescence thermosensitivity. We further demonstrated that the N, S co-doped C-dots could be commendably utilized as a sensitive and effective fluorescent sensor for the determination of temperature, and the detection of erlotinib based on the inner filter effect (IFE), which would broaden the applications of carbon-based nanoparticles significantly.

D-glucose monohydrate and various amino acids were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cysteamine hydrochloride was obtained from Energy Chemical Technology Corporation (Shanghai, China). Erlotinib drug substance was supported by Haoran Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade or the best grade commercially available. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra were performed on a UV-2100 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). All luminescence spectra were measured with a FluoroMax-4C fluorescence (FL) spectrophotometer (Horiba JY, France). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was obtained using a IRAffinity-1S spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) were carried out in a FEI Tecnai G2F20 high-resolution transmission electron microscope with 200 kv acceleration voltage (FEI, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were surveyed on a PHI 5000 Versaprobe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a monochromic Al Ka source (150W, 15kV, 1486.7eV) under vacuum pressure (PHI, Japan). All optical records were measured under stable ambient conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The N, S co-doped C-dots were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment using D-glucose as carbon source and cysteamine hydrochloride as both sulfur and nitrogen sources. Typically, 0.02g D-glucose monohydrate and 0.1g cysteamine hydrochloride were dissolved in 15mL water under ultrasonic. Subsequently, the mixture solution was transferred into a 20mL Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave, heated at 200°C for 12h, and automatically cooled to room temperature. The crude product was centrifuged at 12000rpm for 15min to discard the excess and large-sized deposits, and then purified with a silica column chromatography using CH3OH/CH2Cl2 (2:5, v/v) as eluents. After evaporating the solvent and further drying under vacuum, six purified C-dots samples with blue and green fluorescence were obtained (inset in Fig. 1 a), and the N, S co-doped C-dots with bright blue fluorescence were finally chosen for all further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of erlotinib using N, S co-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: For the determination of erlotinib in water samples, the assay solution of N, S co-doped C-dots (150μgmL−1) was prepared by diluting stock solution (approximate 1.00 mgmL−1) with phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH=4.0). Subsequently, 10μL various concentrations of erlotinib solution were added into 3mL assay solution and kept at a stable room temperature for 10min to achieve the completion of the reaction. Finally, the fluorescence intensity (F, C-dots solution after adding erlotinib) was recorded by a FL spectrophotometer under 340nm excitation wavelength. Simultaneously the fluorescence intensity of blank test (F0, C-dots solution without adding erlotinib) was measured under the same conditions.

To obtain the N, S co-doped C-dots with a high fluorescence quantum yield (QY), the L9(34) orthogonal design was applied to optimize synthetic parameters including the amounts of reactants, reaction time, and reaction temperature. This design allows to evaluate the effects of four factors with nine experiments, each of which is set at three levels. According to the previous procedures [30], the quantum yields of N, S co-doped C-dots under different variables were measured by using quinine sulfate as the reference. As summarized in Fig. S1, the results indicate that four factors play critical roles in the fabrication of N, S co-doped C-dots, and the influence levels are as follows: amounts of cysteamine hydrochloride > reaction temperature > reaction time > amounts of D-glucose monohydrate. Therefore, the optimum synthesis conditions of N, S co-doped C-dots were obtained: 0.02g D-glucose monohydrate and 0.10g cysteamine hydrochloride dissolved into 15mL ultrapure water were reacted at 200°C for 12h, and the QY of as-synthesized N, S co-doped C-dots was estimated to be ∼16.2%.

The typical UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of as-obtained N, S co-doped C-dots were researched. Two broad absorption bands around 266 and 332nm are observed in the absorption spectrum of N, S co-doped C-dots (Fig. 1a, curve a), which are ascribed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and n-π* transition of CO bond, respectively [31]. The N, S co-doped C-dots without any further modification display strong bluish fluorescence under a UV lamp (365nm, center) (inset in Fig. 1a), and the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 340 and 408nm (Fig. 1a, curve b and c) are observed respectively. Furthermore, the N, S co-doped C-dots exhibit the excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence behavior, that the emission peak is the piecemeal red-shift as the excitation wavelength increases (Fig. 1b). The maximum fluorescence emission spectrum can be obtained under 340nm excitation wavelength, which is chosen as the optimum excitation wavelength for the following experiments. The TEM image is depicted in Fig. 1c, clearly revealing that the as-prepared N, S co-doped C-dots are well monodispersed and nearly spherical morphology with some irregularities. The representative image of an individual nanoparticle displays high crystallinity structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21nm (inset of Fig. 1c), which attributes to the diffraction plane of graphitic (sp2) carbon [32]. The EDX analysis (Fig. S2) indicates the existence of four elemental components (C, O, N and S) in our nanoparticles. Diffuse ring pattern acquired from SAED confirms the N, S co-doped C-dots possess certain crystal lattice (inset in Fig. 2S), which is consistent with the HRTEM results.

Meanwhile, the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1d) was performed to determine the surface functional groups present on the N, S co-doped C-dots. The broad absorption bands in the 3100–3500cm−1 range are attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H. The sharp peaks at 1664 and 1377cm−1 are the characteristic absorption bands of −CONH- and N-H, respectively, indicating the successful adulteration of nitrogen atoms in the C-Dots [33]. An obvious peak at 1558cm−1 is identified as the bending vibration of CC. The low-intensity peaks at 400–1450cm−1 can be assigned to the bending vibrations of C-H bond [34]. In this region, two peaks at 1047 and 1016cm−1 can be easily identified that correspond to the stretching vibrations of C-O and C-O-C band, and the peak at 1195cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of C-N or C-S bond [35,36]. Furthermore, the elemental composition and chemical states of as-synthesized N, S co-doped C-dots were characterized by XPS analyses. Four typical predominant peaks around 163.08, 284.08, 400.08, and 531.08eV of the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2 a) correspond to S2p, C1s, N1s, and O1s [35,37,38], respectively, confirming that the nitrogen and sulfur have been doped into the C-dots successfully. Specifically, the deconvolution of the C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) can be fitted into four component peaks at 284.7, 285.8, 286.4, and 288.6eV, which are attributed to C-C/CC, C-N/S, C-O, and CO, respectively. Three distinctive peaks at 399.4, 400.3, and 401.4eV in the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) represent the C-N-C, N-(C)3, and N-H groups, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) displays two characteristic peaks at 531.3 and 532.4eV, which can be assigned to the CO and C-O-C/C-OH groups, respectively. As for the S2p spectrum (Fig. 2e), the main peaks centered at 162.5 and 163.6eV originated from the −C-S- (S2p, 3/2 and S2p, 1/2) covalent bond due to the spin-orbit couplings [38]. The XPS analyses agree well with the FTIR results, both of which demonstrate that the existence of hydrophilic functional groups makes these nanomaterials represent excellent water-solubility.

Interestingly, as illustrated in Fig. 3 a, the as-prepared N, S co-doped C-dots exhibited the temperature-dependent fluorescence that gradually reduced with the increase of ambient temperature varying from 5 to 75°C. There is an excellent linear relationship between the logarithm of quenching efficiency (F0/F) and the temperature (inset in Fig. 3a). The calibration curve can be depicted as the following equation:
Ln (F0/F)=0.0148T−0.0689 (R2 =0.9989,T: 5–75°C)
Where F0 and F are the FL intensities of N, S co-doped C-dots at 5 and T°C respectively, and T represents the temperature. To verify the reversibility and restorability of fluorescence thermosensitivity, we investigated the fluorescence variations via heating and re-cooling the N, S co-doped C-dots in a fixed temperature range. As shown in Fig. 3b, it was clearly observed that the fluorescence of C-dots presented a monotonous decrease at higher temperature, however, upon re-cooling to 5°C, apparently resumed the initial value around. Moreover, the temperature cycling tests (Fig. S3) revealed that the N, S co-doped C-dots owned reversible and restorable fluorescence properties no matter how the temperature (T: 5–75°C) changed. According to the previous literatures [39,40], it could be attributed that the photoexcited electron relaxation, including radiative relaxation, nonradiative relaxation and thermally activated trapping in surface and impurity states, occurred in the heating process of N, S co-doped C-dots, while the re-cooling fluorescent C-dots appeared opposite performances. This reversible character suggested that the N, S co-doped C-dots could perform self-reference fluorescence nanothermometry and be great potentially applied in the temperature sensor.

When the as-synthesized N, S co-doped C-dots were performed in the presence of erlotinib, an apparent fluorescence quenching was observed (Fig. S4). To make the N, S co-doped C-dots as a fluorescent nanoprobe for erlotinib detection with high sensitivity and relatively wide linear range, some parameters of the sensing system were optimized including the amounts of N, S co-doped C-dots, medium pH, temperature, and reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of the amounts of N, S co-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The initial fluorescence intensity (F0) and fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) of the detection system are closely associated with the amounts of fluorescent C-dots. The effect of N, S co-doped C-dots concentration in the range of 25.05-200.4μgmL−1 was investigated. As presented in Fig. 4 a and Fig. S5, the F0 gradually increased with the amounts of N, S co-doped C-dots, while the (F0-F)/F0 achieved the highest value when the C-dots concentration was 150.3μgmL−1. Therefore, 150.3μgmL−1 was taken as the optimized dosage condition for the following study.

The pH of the medium intensively influences the F0 and (F0-F)/F0 of fluorescent C-dots [41,42], so it is critical to research the development of fluorescence assay. As shown in Fig. 4b and Fig. S6, the F0 and (F0-F)/F0 increased rapidly with varying the pH values from 1.0 to 4.0 whereas decreased gradually in the range of 4.0 and 11.0, suggesting that the fluorescence properties of the sensing system strongly depend on the pH values of the medium. As a result, the PBS buffer of pH 4.0 was determined for erlotinib detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of temperature and reaction time
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure the consistency of the entire experiment, it is important to keep the fluorescence signal stable [42]. The fluorescence curves of the system at different temperatures were presented in Fig. 3a. The FL intensities gradually reduced with the increase of the temperature of the system, both of which presented a certain linear relationship. On the other hand, as depicted in Fig. S7, no significant changes in the (F0-F)/F0 were observed after a reaction time of 10min, indicating that the interaction could achieve the balance after 10min. Consequently, the FL intensities of the sensing system were recorded after adding erlotinib for the quenching process of 10min under a stable ambient temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of erlotinib using N, S co-doped C-dots as fluorescent probe

ce:section.ce:para:
Under optimum conditions discussed above, the analytical performances of the sensing system were investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, the FL intensities of N, S co-doped C-dots at 408nm are highly sensitive to erlotinib, and gradually decrease with increasing analyte concentration. There is a good linear correlation between the logarithm of quenching efficiency (F0/F) and the concentration of erlotinib ranging from 0.33 to 13.33μgmL−1 with the following equation:
3.6.1 Ln (F0/F)=0.093C+0.0429 (R2 =0.9977)
(where F0 and F are the FL intensities of N, S co-doped C-dots in the absence and presence of erlotinib respectively, and C represents the concentration of erlotinib). The detection limit of erlotinib was calculated to be 0.027μg·mL−1 based on the signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (S/N=3) [43]. As shown in Table 1 , compared with other analytical techniques reported previously, the N, S co-doped C-dots as a fluorescent nanoprobe also exhibited excellent detection performances toward erlotinib, such as wide linear range and low detection limit. Moreover, the carbon-based nanoparticles are very facile and low-cost, indicating their overwhelmingly feasible application for the determination of erlotinib.

To evaluate the selectivity of fluorescent sensor, we carried out the interference experiments of coexisting substances including various amino acids, metal ions, glucose, ascorbic acid, and some other drugs. As shown in Fig. 4d and Fig. S8a, all these common amino acids and metal ions have no significant interference (fluorescence quenching or enhancement) in determination of erlotinib except for Fe3+ and Cd2+. Moreover, the glucose and ascorbic acid that generally existed in serum, and some other drugs containing similar structured analytes of erlotinib (such as imatinib, gefitinib and dasatinib), also have little interfering effects toward erlotinib (Fig. S8b). All these results indicate that the fluorescent nanoprobe can be highly selective for erlotinib detection, and provide credible results even under high concentration of interfering substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery studies and real sample assay
ce:section.ce:para: Accuracy is a critical parameter to assess the analytical method [44]. To investigate the potential practical applications of the proposed method, we performed a recovery assay that monitored erlotinib in river water and human serum samples through adding standard erlotinib samples. As depicted in Table 2 , the recoveries of different concentration samples prepared independently satisfy analytical requirements, demonstrating that the proposed nanosensor can restrict the interference effects and be enough accurate for real sample analyses.

Enlightened by previous studies, the hypotheses of fluorescence quenching mechanism mainly contain the inner filter effect, energy or charge transfer, and ground-state complex formation [45–48]. To further investigate the possible mechanism of the fluorescence response of N, S co-doped C-dots to erlotinib, we carried out the following experiments. Generally, the analyte and fluorescent C-dots can form the complex through the amino and carboxyl functional groups, which subsequently induces the fluorescence quenching of C-dots by the electrostatic attractive interaction. As shown in Fig. 5 a, UV–vis absorption spectrum of the reaction solution of C-dots and erlotinib produced no new absorption bands that basically agreed with the superposition data, namely there should be not the complex formation between the C-dots and erlotinib. Furthermore, explained by the FT-IR characterization (Fig. S9), it could be observed that the FT-IR spectra have little changes before and after adding erlotinib in the C-dots system, specially the characteristic absorption peak of N-H. In addition, the PBS buffer of pH 4.0 was employed in the sensing system, which might induce amino protonation, and thus the electrostatic attractive interaction for complex formation did not exist.

Meanwhile, as illustrated in Fig. 5b, the FL emission spectrum of C-dots (red curve) slightly overlapped with the absorption spectrum of erlotinib (black curve), but not with the FL excitation spectrum of erlotinib (blue curve), suggesting that the detection system could not occur the fluorescence resonance energy transfer. However, UV–vis absorption spectrum of erlotinib at approximate 340nm (Fig. 5b, black curve) has considerable spectral overlap with the FL excitation spectra of C-dots (Fig. 5b, green curve), so the excitation of C-dots is significantly interfered or weakened because of their competitive absorption, ensuring that the quenching process should be caused by the IFE that is known to arise from the absorption of the excited and/or emitted light by absorbers in the sensing system [49,50]. To further demonstrate this process, we then recorded the absorption spectra of fluorescent C-dots at 340nm enhanced by different concentrations of erlotinib, and monitored the fluorescence changes of the system. As shown in Fig. 4c, the continued addition of erlotinib induced a regular decrease in the FL intensity accompanied by a trivial blue-shift in the emission spectra. Simultaneously, UV–vis absorption (Fig. 5c) of the reaction system at 340nm gradually increased with the increment of erlotinib concentration, and a good linear correlation (Fig. 5d) between them was obtained, which again demonstrated the quenching process of the IFE-based fluorescence assay (Mechanism diagram is depicted in Fig. 5e).

In summary, the novel dual-functional N, S co-doped C-dots, fabricated by a facile and economical hydrothermal strategy, have been developed and utilized as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the temperature and erlotinib detection. The as-prepared N, S co-doped C-dots display superior optical performances, good water-solubility, and remarkable fluorescence thermosensitivity. More notably, the proposed nanosensor not only manifests reversible and restorable fluorescence properties toward the temperature, but also possesses high sensitivity and good selectivity for erlotinib detection in real samples. It is postulated that the erlotinib-mediated fluorescence quenching is attributed to the IFE that involves a competitive absorption. This work could be an important step to widen the investigation and application of carbon-based nanomaterials for the temperature and drug sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21507165).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.08.153.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dots for fluorescent biosensing and bio-imaging applications
Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots and its potential as analysis of four heterocyclic aromatic amines
Facile synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots and application for Fe(III) ions detection and cell imaging
A near-infrared-fluorescence-quenched gold-nanoparticle imaging probe for in vivo drug screening and protease activity determination
Application of nanoparticles in electrochemical sensors and biosensors
Hollow manganese oxide nanoparticles as multifunctional agents for magnetic resonance imaging and drug delivery
Bioconjugated quantum dots as fluorescent probes for bioanalytical applications
The role of helper lipids in lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) designed for oligonucleotide delivery
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Luminescent graphene quantum dots as new fluorescent materials for environmental and biological applications
A review on syntheses properties, characterization and bioanalytical applications of fluorescent carbon dots
Solvent-free synthesis of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from glutathione for highly selective and sensitive detection of mercury(II) ions
Fe3O4/carbon quantum dots hybrid nanoflowers for highly active and recyclable visible-light driven photocatalyst
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning sensors, and bioimaging
A simple turn on fluorescent sensor for the selective detection of thiamine using coconut water derived luminescent carbon dots
Hollow luminescent carbon dots for drug delivery
Developing organic and inorganic nanomedicine for cancer therapy
Aqueous phase synthesis of highly luminescent, nitrogen-doped carbon dots and their application as bioimaging agents
Treatment of non-small-cell lung cancer with erlotinib or gefitinib
Development and validation of a spectrofluorimetric method for the determination of erlotinib in spiked human plasma
Validated stability indicating chromatographic method for the assay of erlotinib active pharmaceutical ingredient
HPLC-DAD protein kinase inhibitor analysis in human serum
Determination of erlotinib in rabbit plasma by liquid chromatography mass spectrometry
Simultaneous determination of nine tyrosine kinase inhibitors by 96-well solid-phase extraction and ultra-performance LC/MS-MS
Quantitative determination of erlotinib and O-desmethyl erlotinib in human EDTA plasma and lung tumor tissue
Simultaneous determination of erlotinib and metabolites in human urine using capillary electrophoresis
Simultaneous determination of erlotinib and tamoxifen in rat plasma using UPLC-MS/MS: Application to pharmacokinetic interaction studies
Determination of the fluorescence quantum yield of quantum dots: suitable procedures and achievable uncertainties
Influence of doping and temperature on solvatochromic shifts in optical spectra of carbon dots
High-yield and high-solubility nitrogen-doped carbon dots: formation, fluorescence mechanism and imaging application
Nanosensor composed of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and gold nanoparticles for highly selective detection of cysteine with multiple signals
In situ building of a nanoprobe based on fluorescent carbon dots for methylmercury detection
Luminescent S-doped carbon dots: an emergent architecture for multimodal applications
Synthesis of a carbon-dot-based photoluminescent probe for selective and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in water and living cells
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg(II) ions
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence
One-step spontaneous synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with thermosensitivity from polyethylene glycol
Synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots via simple acid hydrolysis of bovine serum albumin and its potential as sensitive sensing probe for lead(II) ions
Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots for selective detection of tartrazine in food samples
Highly sensitive and flexible ammonia sensor based on S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots/polyaniline hybrid at room temperature
A highly sensitive fluorescent probe for detection of benzenethiols in environmental samples and living cells
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles for the fluorescent detection of metal ions
Facile synthesis of N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon nanodots for fluorescent resonance energy transfer recognition of methotrexate with high sensitivity and selectivity
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: A low-temperature trypsin-assisted preparation and Fe3+ sensing
Facile and sensitive fluorescence sensing of alkaline phosphatase activity with photoluminescent carbon dots based on inner filter effect
Turn-on fluorescent detection of cyanide based on the inner filter effect of silver nanoparticles
An on-off-on fluorescent nanoprobe for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dot

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhengwei Zhang currently is a pharmaceutical analysis researcher at China Pharmaceutical University. His research focuses on synthesis of novel materials and application in drug analysis.
Yanhua Liu is currently a pharmaceutical analysis researcher at China Pharmaceutical University. Her research focuses on drug spectral analysis.
Zhengyu Yan is currently a pharmaceutical analysis researcher at China Pharmaceutical University. Her research focuses on synthesis of novel materials and application in drug analysis.
Jianqiu Chen is currently a pharmaceutical analysis researcher a at China Pharmaceutical University. His research focuses on synthesis of novel materials and application in drug analysis.
